parsed_goal.objective: Optimize LLZO dopant concentration and temperature to maximize ionic conductivity while preserving phase stability.

studies.title: Improving the Ionic Conductivity of the LLZO–LZO Thin Film through Indium Doping
studies.authors: Zongkai Yan, Yu Song, Shuai Wu, Yongmin Wu
studies.abstract: A solid-state electrolyte with an ionic conductivity comparable to that of a liquid electrolyte is demanded of all-solid-state lithium-ion batteries. Li7La3Zr2O12 (LLZO) is considered to be a promising candidate due to its good thermal stability, high ionic conductivity, and wide electrochemical window. However, the synthesis of a stable cubic-phase LLZO thin film with enhanced densification at a relatively low thermal treatment temperature is yet to be developed. Indium is predicted to be a possible dopant to stabilize the cubic-phase LLZO (c-LLZO). Herein, via a nanolayer stacking process, a LLZO–Li2CO3–In2O3 multilayer solid electrolyte precursor was obtained. After thermal annealing at different temperatures, the effects of indium doping on the formation of c-LLZO and the ionic conductivities of the prepared LLZO–LZO thin film were systematically investigated. The highest ionic conductivity of 9.6 × 10−6 S·cm–1 was obtained at an annealing temperature of 800 °C because the incorporation of indium promoted the formation of c-LLZO and the highly conductive LLZO–LZO interfaces. At the end, a model of LLZO–LZO interface-enhancing ionic conductivity was proposed. This work provides a new approach for the development of low-temperature LLZO-based, solid-state thin-film batteries.

studies.title: Effect of Dopant Concentration in Li<sub>7</sub>La<sub>3</sub>Zr<sub>2</sub>O<sub>12</sub>(LLZO) on Performance of Thermal Battery
studies.authors: Tae-Young Ahn, Heesook Roh, Hyungu Kang, Jiyoun Kim
studies.abstract: A thermal battery is a type of reserved battery that operates by melting a mixture of electrolytes to form the battery's functioning components. Due to its ability to function at high temperatures (500°C), it is primarily used as a power source for high-performance equipment, with much higher ionic conductivity than lithium-ion batteries. Furthermore, at room temperature, the molten salt exists in a solid state with little to no ionic conductivity, making it ideal for use in guided weapons that require activation when necessary without self-discharge. However, in order to be used in weapons that operate in a rotating environment such as projectiles, improvements are needed as the molten salt exists in a liquid state at operating high temperatures. Additionally, ceramic binders such as MgO are used to prevent leakage of the molten salt, but this factor increases the resistance of the battery. A LLZO as solid electrolyte, being excellent in thermal stability and remaining solid at the operating temperatures of thermal battery, could improve stability. LLZO is known to exist two kinds of crystal structures, with the cubic crystal structure known to have high ionic conductivity. It has been reported that the cubic crystal structure can be obtained through doping. A lot of research group has been studied within the temperature range used for lithium ion batteries; therefore, we aimed to select the optimal dopant concentration at high temperatures to improve the discharge performance of thermal batteries. The cubic structure LLZO was synthesized by using Ta, known for its excellent ion conductivity, as a dopant for solid-state transformation. As the amount of Ta dopant increased(Li 7−x La 3 Zr 2−x Ta x O 12 , x = 0.0–0.6), all X-ray diffraction (XRD) results of the obtained LLZO confirmed a cubic crystal structure. The discharge test results confirm that Ta dopant exhibits the best discharge performance when x=0.5, as shown in the figure 1. Although FeS 2 is commonly used as a cathode material in thermal batteries, its performance is limited by its low operating voltage. In this study, the performance of solid-state electrolytes was evaluated using FeF 3 as a cathode material, known as promising candidates for high voltage. We also present findings to elucidate the performance variation of thermal batteries, which results from the addition of eutectic salts, while utilizing FeF 3 as the cathode material and Ta-doped LLZO as the electrolyte. Figure 1

studies.title: Unraveling the Role of Aluminum in Boosting Lithium-Ionic Conductivity of LLZO
studies.authors: Md Mozammal Raju, Yi Ding, Qifeng Zhang
studies.abstract: The development of high-performance solid electrolytes is critical to advancing solid-state lithium-ion batteries (SSBs), with lithium lanthanum zirconium oxide (LLZO) emerging as a leading candidate due to its chemical stability and wide electrochemical window. In this study, we systematically investigated the effects of cation dopants, including aluminum (Al3+), tantalum (Ta5+), gallium (Ga3+), and rubidium (Rb+), on the structural, electronic, and ionic transport properties of LLZO using density functional theory (DFT) and ab initio molecular dynamics (AIMD) simulations. It appeared that, among all simulated results, Al-LLZO exhibits the highest ionic conductivity of 1.439 × 10−2 S/cm with reduced activation energy of 0.138 eV, driven by enhanced lithium vacancy concentrations and preserved cubic-phase stability. Ta-LLZO follows, with a conductivity of 7.12 × 10−3 S/cm, while Ga-LLZO and Rb-LLZO provide moderate conductivity of 3.73 × 10−3 S/cm and 3.32 × 10−3 S/cm, respectively. Charge density analysis reveals that Al and Ta dopants facilitate smoother lithium-ion migration by minimizing electrostatic barriers. Furthermore, Al-LLZO demonstrates low electronic conductivity (1.72 × 10−8 S/cm) and favorable binding energy, mitigating dendrite formation risks. Comparative evaluations of radial distribution functions (RDFs) and XRD patterns confirm the structural integrity of doped systems. Overall, Al emerges as the most effective and economically viable dopant, optimizing LLZO for scalable, durable, and high-conductivity solid-state batteries.

studies.title: Enhanced ionic conductivity of Ga/Nb Co-doped LLZO ceramic electrolyte via sintering regime optimization
studies.authors: Jie Zhao, Ming Guan, Can Cui, Saifang Huang
studies.abstract: To enhance the room-temperature lithium-ion conductivity and interfacial stability of Li 7 La 3 Zr 2 O[Formula: see text] (LLZO) solid-state electrolytes, a co-doping strategy with Ga[Formula: see text] and Nb[Formula: see text] was employed. By systematically optimizing the sintering protocol — including heating rate, temperature, and dwell time — highly dense and phase-pure cubic-phase LLZO ceramics were successfully synthesized. The co-doping approach effectively stabilized the cubic garnet structure and regulated the lattice framework to facilitate Li[Formula: see text] transport pathways. Under optimized conditions, compared to undoped LLZO (0.31 mS cm[Formula: see text], 0.362 eV), the co-doped ceramic exhibited significantly enhanced total ionic conductivity (0.81 mS cm[Formula: see text]) and reduced activation energy (0.207 eV), confirming the superior transport performance enabled by the co-doping strategy. Furthermore, the electrolyte showed good wettability and interfacial compatibility with metallic lithium, enabling stable cycling performance in symmetric Li——Ga/Nb-LLZO——Li cells over short durations. These results confirm the practical potential of the co-doped LLZO electrolyte in all-solid-state lithium batteries. This study provides a viable pathway for the material design and processing of high-performance LLZO-based solid electrolytes.

studies.title: Fracture Toughness, Ionic Conductivity, and Low‐Temperature Phase Stability of Tetragonal Zirconia Codoped with Yttria and Niobium Oxide
studies.authors: Dae‐Joon Kim, Hyung‐Jin Jung, Joo‐Wung Jang, Hong‐Lim Lee
studies.abstract: Tetragonal zirconia (t‐ZrO 2 ) solid solutions were prepared with additions of up to 1.5 mol% of niobium oxide (Nb 2 O 5 ) into 3‐mol%‐yttria‐stabilized t‐ZrO 2 (3Y‐TZP). The influence of pentavalent cation doping on fracture toughness, ionic conductivity, and the tetragonal‐to‐monoclinic phase transformation in the temperature range of 120°‐210°C was investigated. Fracture toughness and ionic conductivity increased and decreased, respectively, as the Nb 2 O 5 content increased, which indicated that the annihilation of oxygen vacancies in 3Y‐TZP was responsible for the instability of the t‐ZrO 2 lattice. The activation enthalpy related to the conductivity was ~83 kJ/mol, regardless of the dopant content, which was consistent with that for the low‐temperature degradation of 3Y‐TZP doped with Nb 2 O 5 . Degradation under an applied electric field occurred only on the specimen surface that was in contact with the anode, which suggests that depletion of the oxygen vacancies led to the degradation. The identical activation enthalpies and the involvement of the vacancy migration in both processes fortified the belief that the low‐temperature degradation of yttria‐stabilized t‐ZrO 2 is attributed to oxygen vacancy diffusion.

The influence of pentavalent cation doping on fracture toughness, ionic conductivity, and the tetragonal‐to‐monoclinic phase transformation in the temperature range of 120°‐210°C was investigated. Fracture toughness and ionic conductivity increased and decreased, respectively, as the Nb 2 O 5 content increased, which indicated that the annihilation of oxygen vacancies in 3Y‐TZP was responsible for the instability of the t‐ZrO 2 lattice. The activation enthalpy related to the conductivity was ~83 kJ/mol, regardless of the dopant content, which was consistent with that for the low‐temperature degradation of 3Y‐TZP doped with Nb 2 O 5 . Degradation under an applied electric field occurred only on the specimen surface that was in contact with the anode, which suggests that depletion of the oxygen vacancies led to the degradation. The identical activation enthalpies and the involvement of the vacancy migration in both processes fortified the belief that the low‐temperature degradation of yttria‐stabilized t‐ZrO 2 is attributed to oxygen vacancy diffusion.

extensions.description: Increase HELIX_SIM_N_REPLICATES for Monte Carlo; set HELIX_SIM_NOISE for relative log-normal noise.

hypothesis.statement: Evidence indicates conductivity, dopant, ionic strongly influences the target outcome.

experiment_ir.summary: The experiment IR artifact defines a literature-conditioned grid design with 42 runs varying LLZO dopant concentration from 0.468 to 7.66 mol% and temperature from 298 to 393 °C to maximize ionic conductivity and phase stability. It uses a single replicate per design point with a lognormal noise model and bases its hypothesis on evidence from 5 studies with a mean relevance of 0.6336. Validation passed with no errors or warnings, and the analysis plan includes response surface scanning and extrema detection.

feasibility_report.summary: The feasibility report indicates the experiment IR compiled for optimizing LLZO dopant concentration and temperature passed validation with warnings, specifically noting that high-temperature cells overlap with literature-reported phase stability concerns.

value_score.summary: The value_score artifact reports a redundancy score of 6.1 and a novelty score of 4.9, indicating moderate overlap with prior work but some new insights. The expected information gain is 0.8174, supporting the experiment's potential to improve understanding. The overall experiment value is 0.7852 with a low sigma spread coefficient of variation at 0.12974, reflecting consistent and feasible optimization of LLZO dopant concentration and temperature for ionic conductivity and phase stability.

protocol.summary: Protocol P-RUN-20260426165221014598 defines a 6-step experimental workflow including powder preparation, ball milling, sintering, impedance measurement, structural validation, and data export. The design space explores 42 points varying LLZO dopant concentration (fraction_mol_pct) and temperature (temperature_c) to optimize ionic conductivity while maintaining phase stability.

schedule.summary: The schedule SCH-RUN-20260426165221014598 plans 42 design points over 14 hours with 72% resource utilization and 6.2 hours idle time, averaging about 0.25 hours per design point to optimize LLZO dopant concentration and temperature for ionic conductivity and phase stability.

execution_log.summary: The execution completed all 42 steps with a recovery triggered due to low phase_proxy values in some measurements, suggesting rework or narrowing the dopant composition window. Measurements across 42 design points varied dopant concentration from 0.468 to 7.66 mol% and temperature from 298 to 393°C, with ionic conductivity peaking around 4.064 mol% at 373°C (2.56e-2 S/cm) while maintaining phase stability proxies mostly above 0.75. The simulation used 5 literature studies with a mean relevance of 0.63 and included a medium-fidelity plugin with noise scale 0.06 and single replicates per condition.

failure_recovery_plan.summary: The failure recovery plan applied the 'narrow_composition_window' strategy due to surrogate phase_proxy values dipping below threshold at edge compositions, affecting 8 design indices (0-6, 8). This recovery aimed to preserve phase stability by restricting the dopant concentration range after execution plugin flagged the issue.

validation_report.summary: The validation_report artifact shows a repaired validation_status with 42 validated_records. Four columns were mapped for analysis: temperature_c to temperature, sigma_S_cm_mean to conductivity, phase_proxy_mean to phase_quality_proxy, and fraction_mol_pct to composition_fraction. Recovery was applied during runtime, enabling successful measurement bundle validation aligned with the user goal to optimize LLZO dopant concentration and temperature for ionic conductivity and phase stability.

interpretation.summary: The artifact identifies the optimal LLZO dopant concentration at 4.064 mol% and temperature 373 °C, achieving the highest ionic conductivity of approximately 0.0256 S/cm. A stability risk is noted at a lower dopant level of 0.468 mol% and 333 °C with a phase proxy mean of 0.7438. The inference relies on a literature-conditioned surrogate model with epistemic uncertainty due to data limitations and noise.

next_experiment_recommendation.summary: The recommendation suggests refining LLZO dopant concentration near 4.06 mol% within a 3.28–4.85 mol% window and adding temperature points around 373.0 °C to optimize ionic conductivity while preserving phase stability. The expected information gain is 0.772 with a moderate risk level of 0.292. Three anchor studies are referenced by DOIs to support this guidance.

memory_update.summary: The memory_update artifact confirms that the experiment memory was successfully updated with one record (RUN-20260426165221014598). The run state is NEXT_EXPERIMENT_RECOMMENDED, reflecting a decision to proceed with further optimization of LLZO dopant concentration and temperature based on validated measurements and recovery actions applied during execution.

report.summary: The report identifies the highest ionic conductivity of approximately 0.0256 S/cm at a dopant concentration of 4.064 mol% and temperature of 373°C, based on 42 measurements from literature-conditioned simulations. Recovery policies were applied following an execution plugin error, and the run state recommends a next experiment to further optimize LLZO dopant concentration and temperature while preserving phase stability.

issues: High-temperature cells overlap with literature stability concerns.

planning.summary: The pipeline generated a protocol to optimize LLZO dopant concentration (0.468 to 7.66 mol%) and temperature (298 to 393 °C) targeting maximized ionic conductivity and phase stability. A literature-conditioned grid design with 42 points was created, informed by 5 relevant studies with a mean relevance of 0.63, and a hypothesis confidence of 0.63 on dopant influence. The experiment plan includes preparation, execution, validation, and analysis steps using a chemistry_materials plugin, with a 14-hour schedule and 72% resource utilization. This structured approach aims to balance conductivity gains with phase stability, guiding experimental decisions on dopant levels and thermal conditions.

runtime.summary: The pipeline completed 42 steps with recovery applied due to low phase stability proxy at edge dopant concentrations, affecting 8 design points. Measurements across dopant fractions from 0.468 to 7.66 mol% and temperatures 298–393°C showed the highest ionic conductivity of ~0.0256 S/cm at 4.064 mol% dopant and 373°C, with good phase stability (phase proxy ~0.9). The surrogate model, informed by 5 literature studies with mean relevance 0.63, guided narrowing the composition window to optimize conductivity while maintaining phase quality. The recovery and repaired results suggest focusing on compositions near 4 mol% dopant for best trade-off between conductivity and stability, though uncertainty remains due to model epistemic limits and noise.

outcomes.summary: The HelixLabs pipeline modeled the highest ionic conductivity of approximately 0.0256 S/cm at a dopant concentration of 4.064 mol% and 373.0 °C, based on a literature-conditioned surrogate using data from 42 measurements across five source studies. Despite a runtime recovery event, the execution plugin successfully validated measurements and interpreted results, leading to a recommendation to refine experiments around 3.28–4.85 mol% dopant concentration with additional temperature points near 373.0 °C. This targeted refinement aims to optimize LLZO dopant levels and temperature for improved conductivity while maintaining phase stability, supported by an expected information gain of 0.772 and a moderate risk level of 0.292.
